titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 6 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.
In this letter Spender is writing to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution.
This brief letter apologises for the delay between letters, and refers to the successes of the Ulster Division in comparison to the rest of the army.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D1633/1/1/337 6 July My pr I really cannot write
letters now tho' I have time,
I think you will understand.
You too, poor old thing will
also find it hard but I am
anxious to get one line to
say that you know I am safe
though so far. I'm afraid there
may have been 2 days gap
when I know the pr will have
had none too easy a time, but
hope it may have passed quickly
with all her work. If you are with Edward
whe this letter arrives he
will be interested in the enclosed
by Falls which tho' based largely
on what I told him, is very
much better than anything I
could write myself. I have not seen the papers
today so do not know if
the Division has been given
- not its due for that is impossible
-but some small share of the praise
it has earned. I hear on many
sides that other troops recognise
that the Ulstermen did far the
best. I wish John Gough was here
to see how true his appreciation
of these men really was. The French man done much
better than the British up to date
but at 1/10th of the losses. Don't overtax yourself win for
the sake of the Ulster brave; for their
(sa)kes keep yourself fit. Your love W Spender
